title: Genesis 4
subtitle: Cain and Abel: Sin, Murder, and the Diverging Lines

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Cain and Abel

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — Cain and Abel: Two Offerings, One Murder

heb.paragraph: V.3-4: The word minchah simply means gift or tribute — it is the same word used for grain offerings in the Mosaic law. The text does not say explicitly why Cain's offering was rejected and Abel's accepted. The most defensible reading, supported by Hebrews 11:4 ("by faith Abel brought a better offering"), is that Abel's offering reflected genuine faith and devotion while Cain's did not.

V.7: The first occurrence of the word "sin" (chattat) in the Bible. God tells Cain: "sin is crouching at the door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over it." The word chattat also means "sin offering" in the Mosaic law — the same word carries both the reality of sin and the provision for its atonement. The language of sin crouching like an animal echoes the earlier serpent imagery.

V.7: The word used for a predatory animal waiting at a door. Sin is personified as a creature that stalks, waits, and desires to devour. The identical phrase appears in ancient Babylonian demonology for a demon lying in wait at a threshold. God warns Cain: you can master it — but you must choose to.

panels.hist: The offerings of Cain and Abel reflect the two primary ancient livelihoods: cultivation (Cain) and herding (Abel). Both are legitimate vocations; neither is inherently inferior. The distinction between the offerings is theological, not occupational. The ancient world universally practiced the presentation of first-fruits and first-born animals to deity as acknowledgment of dependence and gratitude. Both brothers are participating in a recognised religious act; the difference lies in the heart with which they approach.
panels.ctx: Genesis 4 is the first chapter fully outside Eden — the first chapter of the fallen world. It immediately demonstrates that the fall's consequences extend to the next generation: sin passes from parents to children, not just by example but by the inherited condition of the human heart. Cain's story answers the question implicit at the end of chapter 3: what does fallen humanity do with anger, jealousy, and the experience of God's favour shown to another?

cross.note: Abel offered his sacrifice by faith — and through his faith he still speaks, even though he is dead. Abel is the first member of the great cloud of witnesses.
“Do not be like Cain, who belonged to the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did he murder him? Because his own actions were evil and his brother’s were righteous.”
Jesus names Abel as the first righteous martyr — “from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah” — framing redemptive history as the story of innocent blood shed.
The blood of Jesus “speaks a better word than the blood of Abel” — Abel’s blood cried for justice; Christ’s blood speaks mercy.

notes.note: The rejection of Cain's offering and acceptance of Abel's is the first act of divine discrimination in human history outside the garden. The text does not explain why — but Hebrews 11:4 supplies the answer: "by faith Abel offered a better sacrifice than Cain did." The difference was not the content of the offering (one was agricultural, one was animal) but the disposition of the worshipper. God looks at the heart before he looks at the gift.

God's word to Cain before the murder is one of the most remarkable displays of divine grace in the early chapters of Genesis. God does not condemn Cain; he warns him, invites him to repent, and promises that doing well will restore his standing. The warning — "sin is crouching at the door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over it" — gives Cain full knowledge of his danger and full moral responsibility for what follows. He cannot claim ignorance.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Hebrew text of vv.4-5 draws a significant contrast: "and YHWH looked with favour on Abel and his offering, but on Cain and his offering he did not look with favour." The sequence in each case is person first, then offering — God's acceptance or rejection is of the person before the gift. Sarna notes this is consistent with the broader biblical pattern: God's relationship with the worshipper determines the reception of the worship, not vice versa.

The word chattat (sin) appears here for the first time in Scripture. Its placement is theologically dense: it is both a warning (sin is crouching) and an implicit offer of mercy (you can master it). The same root will later give the sin offering (chattat) in Leviticus — the provision for dealing with precisely this kind of failure. Sarna: "The possibility of overcoming the evil impulse is clearly implied in God's words, placing full moral responsibility on Cain."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Vattar haggid et Qayin — "and she conceived and bore Cain." Eve's exclamation — "I have acquired a man with the help of the LORD" — is programmatic for the chapter. The birth of a son is an act of divine partnership. But the name Cain (qayin, from qanah = to acquire/create) contains an ambiguity: will this created/acquired being be a blessing or a curse? The narrative answers immediately.

God's speech to the angry Cain is the chapter's moral pivot. Alter notes the structure: a rhetorical question (why are you angry?), a conditional promise (if you do well, acceptance), a warning (sin crouches at the door), and an exhortation (you must master it). The speech gives Cain everything he needs to choose differently. The narrative then moves immediately to the murder — a transition of devastating economy. God spoke; Cain chose otherwise.

Why was Cain's offering rejected? Calvin: "The cause of this must be traced to the mind. If the integrity of the heart had been wanting in Cain, his oblation could not have been agreeable to God... as the moral virtue is absent, so the sacrifice itself is vitiated." Calvin refuses to explain the rejection by the content of the offerings (grain vs. animal) — the issue is the spiritual condition of the worshipper. God has no use for external religion divorced from internal devotion.

Calvin on "sin is crouching at the door": "By these words God signifies that Cain would have no excuse if he should fall, because he had the power of governing himself. This truly is an important and even memorable sentence, for it shows that sin proceeds from the free will of man." The warning to Cain is Calvin's proof text for moral responsibility even within a fallen nature: the impulse to sin can be resisted, and failure to resist it is culpable.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "the LORD looked with favour" (wayyisha) discusses the idiom — literally "he lifted his eyes to." The note acknowledges the text does not explain the basis for acceptance/rejection and surveys interpretive options: quality of offering (animal vs. grain), first-fruits vs. leftovers, faith vs. lack of faith. The note favours Hebrews 11:4's faith-basis interpretation as the most consistent with the broader biblical pattern.

The NET note on chattat (sin) notes this is the word's first occurrence in the Bible. The note discusses the personification of sin as a crouching predator (the Hebrew rovets appears in Akkadian for a demon at a threshold) and observes that the divine speech gives Cain complete moral knowledge and responsibility. "You must master it" (timshel in the KJV, which Steinbeck famously discusses in East of Eden) is correctly translated as an imperative — a command, not merely a possibility.

sections.header: Verses 9–16 — God's Judgment on Cain; the Mark

heb.paragraph: V.9: "Am I my brother's keeper?" — one of the most famous questions in world literature. The word shomer (keeper, guardian) echoes the shamar of 2:15: Adam was commissioned to "keep" the garden; Cain refuses to "keep" his brother. The one who should have been a guardian has become a destroyer. The question is rhetorical deflection; God's response makes clear that the answer is: yes, you are.

V.12: The curse on Cain is a double noun, a rhyming pair: na (wandering aimlessly) + nad (staggering, fugitive). The earth will no longer yield to him; he is cut off from the adamah that sustained his vocation as a farmer. He becomes the paradigm of the person without roots, without home, without belonging. The city he builds (v.17) is the fallen world's answer to rootlessness: constructing stability without restoration.

panels.hist: The "mark of Cain" (v.15) has been one of the most misused passages in the history of biblical interpretation — wrongly used to justify racial theories. The text says nothing about the nature of the mark; it is explicitly a sign of divine protection, not degradation. Cain is protected from blood vengeance — the ancient Near Eastern custom of clan retaliation for murder. The irony is that the murderer is protected from murder. This is grace, not curse.
panels.ctx: God's interrogation of Cain follows the same pattern as the interrogation of Adam in chapter 3: a question that already knows the answer, giving the guilty party an opportunity to confess. Cain's "I don't know" is a lie — and God does not accept it. The blood of Abel "cries out" from the ground — the first murder leaves a stain on the earth itself. The ground that resisted the farmer (3:17-19) now absorbs the farmer's brother's blood.

cross.note: After the flood God formally institutes the death penalty for murder, grounding it in the Imago Dei — what Cain violated by killing Abel.
“The LORD watches over you” — YHWH asshomer
Abel’s blood cried for vengeance; Christ’s blood speaks mercy. The contrast between the two brothers becomes a contrast between two covenants.
“Woe to them! They have taken the way of Cain” — Cain becomes a type of godless religious externalism without genuine faith or love.

poi.name: Land of Nod
poi.text: Nod means wandering — the land named for Cain's condition, not a prior geographical feature. "Settled in the land of Nod" is almost paradoxical: the wanderer establishes a city. The city (v.17) is built to create the stability that the curse removed.

notes.note: "Am I my brother's keeper?" is not a serious question but a defiant evasion. Cain lies to God — "I don't know" — knowing full well what he has done. The blood of Abel crying from the ground (v.10) is not merely a metaphor: God hears the voice of the innocent dead. Murder does not end with the death of the victim; it creates an ongoing claim on divine justice. Abel speaks from the grave, and God hears.

The mark placed on Cain for his protection is one of the most counterintuitive acts of grace in Genesis. The murderer is protected from murder. God does not execute Cain; he preserves him — in exile, marked, alienated — but alive. The city Cain builds (v.17) is the fallen world's substitute for Eden: human technology constructing the security and permanence that God alone can provide. It is the beginning of civilisation as an alternative to dependence on God.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Cain's answer — lo yadati ("I do not know") — is the Bible's first recorded lie to God. Sarna notes that the divine interrogation follows the same pattern as Genesis 3: God asks a question, the guilty party evades. The question "where is Abel your brother?" mirrors "where are you?" from 3:9. The pattern of divine-human dialogue after sin is established: God confronts, humanity evades, God renders judgment.

Arur attah min haAdamah — "cursed are you from the ground." The curse on Cain is more severe than the curse on Adam (3:17-19). Adam was cursed in his work with the ground; Cain is cursed from the ground — expelled from the very relationship that defined his vocation. The farmer who killed his brother on the farmland is exiled from farming. The punishment fits the crime with terrible precision.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ha'ach shelcha — "your brother" — God uses the relational term twice in his confrontation: "where is Abel your brother?" and "what have you done? The voice of your brother's blood is crying to me from the ground." The repetition of "brother" hammers at the nature of the crime. Cain did not merely kill a person; he killed his brother. The first murder in human history is a fratricide — which will be the pattern of countless subsequent biblical conflicts.

Gadol avoni mineso — "my punishment is greater than I can bear." Alter notes the ambiguity: avon can mean either "sin" or "punishment." Is Cain saying "my sin is too great to be forgiven" or "my punishment is too great to be borne"? Both readings are grammatically possible. The ambiguity captures something true about the human experience of guilt: the inability to distinguish between the weight of what one has done and the weight of its consequences.

Calvin on "am I my brother's keeper?": "Cain not only denies his guilt but boastingly glorifies himself for the crime. He indirectly charges God with foolishness, as if he had said, 'Thou dost foolishly trouble thyself about the blood of one man.'" The response is not merely evasion but arrogance — Cain challenges the divine right to hold him accountable. This is sin fully developed: not just the act but the proud denial of accountability.

The mark of divine protection on Cain is mercy triumphing over justice. Calvin: "God, by marking Cain with a certain sign, consulted the public good in restraining the hands of all men from homicide." But more than social utility, the mark is an act of grace: even the murderer is not abandoned by God. The preservation of Cain serves the larger narrative purpose: through the line of Seth (not Cain), the promised seed will come. God uses even the wicked to advance his purposes.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "the voice of your brother's blood is crying to me" explains the Hebrew plural demei (bloods) — often interpreted as indicating multiple voices or the blood of all of Abel's potential descendants crying out. The note connects this to Numbers 35:33 (blood defiles the land) and observes that the earth itself is treated as a moral witness to the murder. The land that opened to receive Abel's blood now refuses to yield its produce to his murderer.

The NET note on the "mark of Cain" is careful to note the text says nothing about the nature of the mark. It is explicitly a sign of divine protection, not of divine curse or condemnation. The note corrects historical misreadings that identified the mark with race or skin colour — the text provides no basis for such identification. The mark is protection; its nature is unknown.

sections.header: Verses 17–26 — The Line of Cain; Seth and the Name of the Lord

heb.paragraph: V.23-24: Lamech's "sword song" — the first recorded poem in the Bible after the birth songs — is a poem of escalating violence. "I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for injuring me. If Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-seven times." He claims the divine protection given to Cain but amplifies it with personal vengeance. Where Cain murdered and was marked for protection, Lamech boasts of murder and claims disproportionate revenge.

V.26: The birth of Enosh (meaning "mortal man," related to the word for weakness or frailty) triggers the note: "at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD." After the descent into violence in Cain's line, the line of Seth initiates worship. The contrast between the two lines — Cain's line producing technology and violence, Seth's line producing worship — frames the rest of the primeval history.

panels.hist: The genealogy of Cain (vv.17-22) is remarkable for what it preserves: the origins of city-building, nomadic herding, music, and metalworking. These are presented without moral evaluation — they are cultural achievements, gifts of human creativity and ingenuity. But they occur in the line of the murderer. Cain's descendants develop civilisation; Seth's descendants develop worship. The two trajectories — cultural achievement and covenant relationship — are the two primary threads of human history from this point forward.
panels.ctx: The chapter ends with a dramatic contrast: Lamech's boast of seventy-seven-fold vengeance (the escalation of Cain's violence to its logical extreme) set against the birth of Enosh and the beginning of worship (the beginning of Seth's counter-movement). The two lines will run parallel through the primeval history: the line of those who build on human power and the line of those who call on the name of the LORD.

cross.note: Jesus overturns Lamech’s seventy-seven-fold vengeance with seventy-seven-fold (or seventy times seven) forgiveness — a direct reversal of Gen 4:24.
“Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved” — the covenantal prayer that began with Seth’s generation becomes the promise of the new covenant age.
Peter quotes Joel 2:32 on Pentecost — the “calling on the name” inaugurated in Genesis 4:26 reaches its fullest expression at the birth of the church.
Luke’s genealogy of Jesus traces through Seth all the way to Adam — confirming the Seth line as the one through which the promised seed travels.

notes.note: The cultural achievements of Cain's line — city-building, animal herding, music, metalworking — are presented as genuine human accomplishments. God did not strip fallen humanity of creativity or cultural capacity. But these achievements occur in the line of the murderer, apart from God. The pattern is set for the entire subsequent history of human civilisation: remarkable creativity and achievement, but built on violence and apart from dependence on God.

The birth of Enosh and the beginning of calling on the name of the LORD is the first indication of the counter-movement to the fall. In the midst of the expanding violence of Cain's line, Seth's descendants begin to worship. This is the spiritual lineage that will run through the primeval history to Abraham, and from Abraham to the promised Seed. The genealogy of chapter 5 will carry this line forward.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Vayiven Kayin ir — "and Cain built a city." The first city in the Bible is built by the first murderer, named after his son. Sarna notes the irony: the man sentenced to be a fugitive and wanderer (na vanad, v.12) builds a fixed settlement. The city is the fallen world's answer to the curse of rootlessness — constructing belonging where God has withheld it. The technology of human civilisation begins as a response to exile.

Lamech's "sword song" is the Bible's earliest recorded poetry and also its first celebration of violence. The poem escalates the protection promised to Cain (sevenfold) to a boasted claim of seventy-sevenfold. Sarna: "This is the first recorded instance of the corruption of poetry in the service of violence — a pattern that will recur throughout human history." The gifts of culture (music, poetry) are immediately co-opted by the pride and violence of the fallen heart.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The genealogy of Cain's line is the Bible's first record of cultural and technological development. Alter notes the structure: each generation is introduced by "he was the father of" (linking invention to lineage), the activities span the range of human civilisation (city, herding, music, metalwork), and the daughters are mentioned — unusual in ancient genealogies, perhaps suggesting the cultural richness of this line. But it culminates in Lamech's murder-boast, not in worship.

The chapter ends with two births and one beginning: Seth (to replace the murdered Abel), Enosh (mortal humanity), and the calling on the name of the LORD. Alter: "The final verse of the chapter sets the terms for the narrative that follows: two human trajectories, a Cainite line of building and violence, and a Sethite line of mortality and worship. The rest of the primeval history traces these two lines toward their collision in the flood."

Lamech's song reveals the natural development of sin when unchecked. Calvin: "We see how, through successive generations, the contagion of sin has been spreading; and as pride always generates cruelty, Lamech breaks out into such ferocity that he not only confesses himself to be a murderer, but loudly and boastingly applauds himself for the crime." The escalation from Cain's single murder to Lamech's proud boast is the pattern of human sin: it grows, justifies itself, and eventually celebrates itself.

The birth of Seth and the beginning of worship is the beginning of the church — the people who call on the name of the LORD. Calvin: "The name of God began then to be eminently celebrated; and that men set up a visible symbol of religion among themselves, so that they might together make a common profession of their faith." The line of Seth is not merely a biological genealogy but the succession of the faithful who maintain the knowledge and worship of God in a world given over to violence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Lamech's song discusses the ambiguity of v.23b — "I have killed a man for wounding me." The Hebrew could be translated as a real past event ("I have killed") or as a conditional boast ("I would kill"). The note favours the past event reading as more consistent with the escalating violence narrative. The note also addresses the number 77: Jesus' use of the same number in Matthew 18:22 (70 x 7 in some manuscripts, 77 in others) is likely a deliberate inversion of the Lamech boast — where Lamech claimed 77-fold revenge, Jesus commands unlimited forgiveness.

The NET note on "began to call on the name of the LORD" discusses whether this marks the beginning of worship generally or specifically the use of the divine name YHWH. The note acknowledges the tension with Exodus 6:3 (where God appears to say the name YHWH was not known until Moses), and surveys harmonising approaches: the name was used before Exodus but not in its full covenantal significance; the Exodus text refers to the full meaning of the name rather than its first use.

ppl.name: Cain
ppl.role: First murderer; builder of the first city
ppl.text: Cain's story is the first post-fall human story — and it is a story of unchecked jealousy, murder, and evasion. God gives him every opportunity: a warning before the murder, an interrogation after it, and a mark of protection in exile. He builds a city — the fallen world's answer to rootlessness. His line produces cultural achievement and escalating violence simultaneously. The NT reads him as a figure of the spiritually dead who hate the righteous (1 John 3:12).

ppl.name: Abel
ppl.text: Abel is the first person in Scripture of whom "faith" is predicated (Heb 11:4). He brought a better offering "by faith" — genuine devotion expressed in genuine sacrifice. He speaks nothing in the narrative; his only speech is his blood crying from the ground. Jesus calls him "righteous Abel," the first of the line of righteous martyrs who are killed by the wicked (Matt 23:35). His blood "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (Heb 12:24) — pointing to the sacrifice that will answer, not merely cry.

ppl.name: Lamech
ppl.role: Cain's descendant; first poet of violence
ppl.text: Lamech is the culmination of the Cainite line before the flood narrative. In him, the violence of Cain reaches its logical extreme: boastful, proportionless, self-aggrandising vengeance. His "sword song" is the Bible's first poetry in the service of pride and violence. He stands as the representative figure of human civilisation built on fallen pride: culturally sophisticated (his sons invent music and metalwork), morally bankrupt.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslation4:7 — chattat rovets (sin crouching)NIVsin is crouching at your door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over itESVsin is crouching at the door. Its desire is contrary to you, but you must rule over itNASBsin is crouching at the door; and its desire is for you, but you must master itKJVsin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over himNoteThe feminine noun chattat (sin) takes a masculine verb (rovets = crouching) — an unusual grammatical feature that may suggest the personification of sin as a masculine predatory figure. The verb timshel ("you must rule") in KJV became famous through Steinbeck's East of Eden.4:26 — "call on the name of the LORD"NIV/ESV/NASBpeople began to call on the name of the LORDNoteThe use of YHWH here vs. Exod 6:3 (where the name seems to be revealed first to Moses) is one of the classic source-critical observations. Conservative interpretation: the name was used earlier but its full covenantal significance was not yet revealed.NIVsin is crouching at the door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over itESVsin is crouching at the door. Its desire is contrary to you, but you must rule over itNASBsin is lurking at the door; and its desire is for you, but you must master itKJVsin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over himNoteESV 'contrary to you' is a notable interpretive choice — reading teshuqah (desire) negatively, as hostile desire to dominate. NIV and NASB read it as simple desire/longing. The same word in 3:16 describes the woman's desire for her husband. KJV's masculine 'him' reflects an older reading of sin as a personified male figure. The Akkadian word rabisu (a crouching demon at doorways) may underlie the imagery.NIVthe LORD put a mark on Cain so that no one who found him would kill himESVthe LORD put a mark on Cain, lest any who found him should attack himNASBthe LORD appointed a sign for Cain, so that no one finding him would kill himNoteNo translation specifies what the mark was — the Hebrew ot means sign or mark but gives no physical description. Ancient interpreters guessed: a letter on his forehead (Rashi), a horn (Targum), a dog companion, a trembling body. The text's silence is deliberate. The mark's function — protection, not punishment — is unambiguous in all versions.

src.title: The Sumerian King List (c.2100 BCE)
src.quote: The Sumerian King List records pre-flood kings with astronomically long reigns, followed by a flood, followed by post-flood kings with shorter (but still very long) reigns. The structure parallels the Genesis genealogies of chapters 5 and 11: long pre-flood lifespans, followed by the flood, followed by declining lifespans.
src.note: The structural parallel suggests that both traditions are working with similar historical memory about a pre-flood era of unusual longevity. Genesis does not simply borrow the structure but reframes it: the Sumerian kings are heroic figures; Genesis' pre-flood figures are primarily defined by their relationship to God.

src.title: The Lamech Texts from Qumran (1QapGen)
src.quote: The Genesis Apocryphon from Qumran (Dead Sea Scrolls) expands the story of Noah but also contains fragmentary material about Lamech and his son, suggesting that the Lamech-Noah genealogy was a subject of intense interest in Second Temple Judaism.
src.note: The Qumran expansion shows that early Jewish readers were fascinated by the Cainite-Sethite distinction and the moral significance of the two genealogies. The contrast between violence (Cain's line) and covenant faithfulness (Seth's line) was the interpretive key for the flood narrative.

rec.who: Philo of Alexandria, On the Posterity of Cain (c.20 BCE)
rec.text: Philo reads the Cain-Abel narrative allegorically: Cain represents the soul that attributes all good to itself (self-love); Abel represents the soul that attributes all good to God. The murder is the victory of self-love over God-love, which Philo reads as the perpetual internal struggle of the soul. The genealogy of Cain is the genealogy of the vices; the line of Seth is the line of the virtues.

rec.who: Augustine, The City of God (c.413-426 AD)
rec.text: Augustine reads Genesis 4 as the founding document of his "two cities" theory. Cain builds the earthly city (the city of man, founded on self-love, culminating in Rome); Abel/Seth represents the heavenly city (founded on the love of God). The two cities — human civilisation organised around self and divine community organised around God — run parallel through history until the final judgment. The Cainite genealogy is the archetype of human political history; the Sethite genealogy is the archetype of the church.

The Cain story has generated enormous literary and philosophical reflection. The mark of Cain has been read as a sign of the murderer's ongoing guilt, of divine mercy, and of the ambiguity of human moral status. The question "am I my brother's keeper?" has become shorthand for the tension between individual freedom and social responsibility in modern ethics. The enduring power of the narrative lies in its psychological precision: jealousy, evasion, God's patient pursuit of the guilty.

rows.label: Two Offerings — 4:1-5
rows.text: Cain (agriculture) and Abel (herding) both offer. Abel's is accepted; Cain's is not. No explicit reason given in text — Heb 11:4 supplies "faith."

rows.label: Warning — 4:6-7
rows.text: Divine speech to Cain: opportunity to repent, warning of sin's power, command to master it. Then silence — narrative moves to murder.

rows.label: Interrogation — 4:9-16
rows.text: Pattern of Genesis 3: divine question, human evasion ("I don't know"), divine judgment, unexpected mercy (mark of protection).

rows.label: Cainite Line — 4:17-24
rows.text: Cultural achievements (city, herding, music, metal) and escalating violence (Lamech's sword song). Civilisation and pride in tandem.

rows.label: Sethite Beginning — 4:25-26
rows.text: Seth replaces Abel; Enosh born; worship begins. The counter-line, the covenant trajectory, is established.

hebtext.note: V.3-4: The word minchah simply means gift or tribute — it is the same word used for grain offerings in the Mosaic law. The text does not say explicitly why Cain's offering was rejected and Abel's accepted. The most defensible reading, supported by Hebrews 11:4 ("by faith Abel brought a better offering"), is that Abel's offering reflected genuine faith and devotion while Cain's did not.

V.7: The first occurrence of the word "sin" (chattat) in the Bible. God tells Cain: "sin is crouching at the door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over it." The word chattat also means "sin offering" in the Mosaic law — the same word carries both the reality of sin and the provision for its atonement. The language of sin crouching like an animal echoes the earlier serpent imagery.

V.7: The word used for a predatory animal waiting at a door. Sin is personified as a creature that stalks, waits, and desires to devour. The identical phrase appears in ancient Babylonian demonology for a demon lying in wait at a threshold. God warns Cain: you can master it — but you must choose to.

V.9: "Am I my brother's keeper?" — one of the most famous questions in world literature. The word shomer (keeper, guardian) echoes the shamar of 2:15: Adam was commissioned to "keep" the garden; Cain refuses to "keep" his brother. The one who should have been a guardian has become a destroyer. The question is rhetorical deflection; God's response makes clear that the answer is: yes, you are.

V.12: The curse on Cain is a double noun, a rhyming pair: na (wandering aimlessly) + nad (staggering, fugitive). The earth will no longer yield to him; he is cut off from the adamah that sustained his vocation as a farmer. He becomes the paradigm of the person without roots, without home, without belonging. The city he builds (v.17) is the fallen world's answer to rootlessness: constructing stability without restoration.

V.23-24: Lamech's "sword song" — the first recorded poem in the Bible after the birth songs — is a poem of escalating violence. "I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for injuring me. If Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-seven times." He claims the divine protection given to Cain but amplifies it with personal vengeance. Where Cain murdered and was marked for protection, Lamech boasts of murder and claims disproportionate revenge.

V.26: The birth of Enosh (meaning "mortal man," related to the word for weakness or frailty) triggers the note: "at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD." After the descent into violence in Cain's line, the line of Seth initiates worship. The contrast between the two lines — Cain's line producing technology and violence, Seth's line producing worship — frames the rest of the primeval history.

thread.text: "Sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment" — Paul's analysis of sin using the law echoes the language of sin crouching and seizing. Cain's experience is universalised in Paul's anthropology.
Jesus names Abel as the first of the righteous martyred by the wicked — establishing the line of prophetic martyrdom that runs from Abel to the prophets to Jesus himself.
The blood of Abel cries from the ground; the blood of Jesus speaks a better word. Abel's blood cries for justice; Christ's blood cries for mercy.
Cain's city-building is the prototype of Babel — human civilisation constructing permanence and security without reference to God. Both culminate in divine judgment.
Lamech claimed 77-fold revenge; Jesus commands 77-fold forgiveness (Matt 18:22). The most precise inversion of a Genesis text in the entire NT teaching of Jesus.

tx.title: qaniti — "I have gotten" or "I have created"?
tx.content: MTqaniti ish et-YHWH: "I have acquired/gotten a man with YHWH." The verb qanah = to acquire, get, possess. Eve's exclamation connects the birth of Cain (qayin) with acquisition. Some (Cassuto) translate "with the help of YHWH"; others read et as "the LORD" (i.e., Eve thinks Cain IS YHWH — a messianic misidentification). The messianic reading is theologically interesting but syntactically strained.
tx.note: Eve's understanding of the proto-evangel (3:15) and the role of childbearing in its fulfillment. Does she think she has already produced the promised seed?

tx.title: sin is crouching at the door — personification or condition?
tx.content: MTchatat robets = sin crouching/lying down. The word chatat (sin/sin-offering) with a masculine participle (robets) while chatat is feminine — a grammatical anomaly. Some read this as sin personified as a demonic figure crouching at the door (cf. Akkadian rabisu = a crouching demon). Others read it simply as the metaphor of sin as a lurking predator.
tx.note: Whether the text is presenting sin as a demonic entity or as a metaphorical description of the temptation dynamic has implications for demonology and the theology of sin.

tx.title: the mark of Cain — what was it?
tx.content: MTThe text says God "put a mark on Cain" (ot = sign/mark). The nature of the mark is entirely unspecified in the text. It is a protective mark, not a mark of shame — designed to prevent vengeance killings. Centuries of speculation have ranged from a physical mark to a divine sign visible only to others.
tx.note: The mark has been tragically misused in the history of interpretation (e.g., to justify racial slavery). The text itself specifies only that it was protective. Its nature is unknown.

debate.title: The "Sons of God" in Context of Cain's Fear (4:14-15)

positions.name: Pre-existing humans
positions.argument: Adam and Eve had children before the recorded births; Cain fears people already alive but not named in the Genesis genealogy.

positions.name: Future children
positions.argument: Cain fears his own future siblings and their descendants. His fear is of what will develop over time; the curse makes him a wanderer before the full population fills the earth.

positions.name: Narrative convention
positions.argument: The narrative does not claim to be an exhaustive record; it assumes a wider population as the story requires. The question of where Cain's wife came from is similarly answered by narrative convention — not all births are recorded.

debate.title: Lamech's Song (4:23-24): Escalating Violence and the Sevenfold Curse

positions.name: Raw boast of escalation
positions.argument: Lamech's song is a bragging, violent celebration of his own revenge — taking Cain's divine protection (sevenfold) and claiming seventy-sevenfold for himself. It represents the escalation of violence in the line of Cain.

positions.name: Cultural-heroic boast
positions.argument: The poem is an example of ancient Near Eastern warrior poetry — culturally sanctioned bragging that doesn't reflect individual psychology so much as tribal identity.

themes.note: Genesis 4 is the fall's first fruit in the next generation: jealousy, murder, evasion, and escalating violence. But it is also the chapter where worship begins (4:26) and where the covenant line of Seth is established. The chapter establishes the two trajectories that will run through the rest of the primeval history: the line of those who build on human strength alone, and the line of those who call on the name of the LORD. The blood of Abel — the first martyr — will speak throughout Scripture until it meets the better blood of the last innocent.
